Kannon Pilgrimage}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Wakayama, Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2024}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Seiganto-ji | native_name = 青岸渡寺 | image = Three-storied Pagoda of Seiganto-ji and Nachi Falls 201808.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Three-storied pagodas with [[Nachi Falls]] in the background | map_type = Japan | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 8 Nachisan, [[:en:Nachikatsuura, Wakayama|Nachikatsuura-chō]], [[Higashimuro District, Wakayama|Higashimuro-gun]], {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|33.6694|135.8898|type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} | deity = [[Cintamani|Nyoirin]] [[Kannon]] (Chintamanicakra) | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = | founded_by = [[Ragyō Shōnin]] | year_completed = 4th century (presumed legendary) | designation1 = WHS | designation1_type = Cultural | designation1_criteria = ii, iii, iv, vi | designation1_date = 2004 <small>(28th {{ill|World Heritage Committee|en|World Heritage Committee|WD=Q1147477|lt=session|1=World Heritage Committee}})</small> | designation1_free1name = Part of | designation1_free1value = {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} | designation1_number = [https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142bis 1142bis] }} {{nihongo|'''Seiganto-ji'''|青岸渡寺|1=Seiganto-ji|qq=draft}}, Temple of Crossing the Blue Shore, is a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was designated a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} in 2004 as part of {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}. According to a legend, it was founded by [[Ragyō Shōnin]], a monk from {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. The temple was purposely built near [[Nachi Falls]], where it may have previously been a site of nature worship. Seiganto-ji, part of the ''[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]]'' shrine complex, is one of the few remaining ''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}'' or [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|shrine temples]] following the forcible separation of Shinto and Buddhism during the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Jungūji|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1126|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=29 September 2010}}</ref>
It is the first stop on the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}} and is also classified as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the Japanese government.{{Citation needed|date=March 2024}}
== History == During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} (313 – 399), according to temple legend, Ragyō Shōnin, a monk from India, came here following the river in search of a suitable place in which to practice his austerities and found [[Nachi Falls|Nachi waterfall]]. After practicing shugyō, Kannon appeared to him at the base of the waterfall and so he built a hermitage dedicated to Kannon in this place.
During the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592 – 628), Shōbutsu Shōnin came here from Yamato in order to undergo austerity practices. At that time he carved a 4 meter high image of Nyoirin Kannon from a single piece of camellia tree. The Hondō (main temple) was built to enshrine this image which became the focus of the Nachi Kannon cult and is the image that is enshrined in the present Nyorindō.
In 988 {{ill|Emperor Kazan|en|Emperor Kazan|WD=Q361000|lt=Emperor Kazan|1=Emperor Kazan}} (花山天皇) (968-1008) visited the Kumano area on his first pilgrimage and, being deeply moved by the image of Kannon, he declared this Temple One of the Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage. It is said that Emperor Kazan completed 1000 days of severe spiritual training under the waterfall, after which he had a vision of Kannon in the form of the kami Kumano Gongen. The kami instructed the emperor to find the priest Butsugan of Hasedera (Temple 8 of the pilgrimage), who helped the emperor to remap out the current pilgrimage route.
Emperor Kazan wrote all the goeika poems that are still used throughout the pilgrimage as sacred hymns. It became a custom in later years for other emperors who went on this pilgrimage to also compose poems of their own for each of the sacred sites.
Because [[:en:Emperor Gotoba of Japan|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239; r. 1183–1198) made the pilgrimage to Kumano 31 times and his successor {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} (1127 – 1192; reigned 1155 – 1158) made it 34 times, the pilgrimage became popular during this era. However, members of court had been coming here for about 400 years prior to this, believing it was near to Kannon's paradise island located to the south of Japan called Fudaraku (Potala in Sanskrit).
The temple buildings, like many of the temples on the pilgrimage route, were burned to the ground by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} during the civil wars of the 16th century. The Nyorindō (Main Temple) was rebuilt in 1587 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the Imperial Regent who unified Japan after the wars. It is typical of [[:en:Momoyama era|Momoyama Era]] style of architecture with a shingled distinctive roof called irimoya. The Nyorindō is heritage listed as a nationally Important Cultural Property.
When the government reinstated the power of the Emperor during the Meiji era (1868 - 1912), an attempt was made to separate Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines that had for over a thousand years shared the same grounds. Because of this, Seigantoji was formally abolished, the priests stripped of their authority and no funding was given to the temple. At that time Seigantoji had three main temples as well as 37 residential and training building. But all that remained after the Meiji Restoration was the Nyorindō and the abbot's quarters. However, gradually over the following century it was slowly rebuilt due to its position as an important part of the Kumano-Nachi syncretic mountain veneration religion of Shugendō.
In 1918, a {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} mound was excavated at the base of the waterfall and found to contain many important archaeological artifacts, including statues, mirrors, altar fittings and Sutra cylinders. These are now displayed in the Ryuhoden (“Treasure Hall”), located next to the Pagoda. These Sutra mounds were created by priests in times of war to hide their treasures but also many items were buried in this way as a result of the belief that the {{ill|Eschatology|en|Eschatology|WD=Q170337|lt=end of the world|1=Eschatology}} was coming at the start of the 10th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/History.htm|title = History of Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
== Buildings and other places of interest within the temple grounds == * Daimonsaka Forest Path * Sanmon (Mountain Gate) * [[Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha shrine]] (heiden) * Homotsuden Treasure Hall * Nyoirindō (The Temple of the Wish-fulfilling Kannon), the main temple (Hondō) * Sanjūdō pagoda (3-storey pagoda) * Nachi-no-Taki (Nachi Waterfall) * Nyohōdō (Hall of Lantern) * Sonsho-in Abbot's Quarters * Waniguchi "Crocodile Mouth" Gong * [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Places.htm|title = Places of Interest at Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>

It is the first stop on the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}} and is also classified as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the Japanese government.{{Citation needed|date=March 2024}}
== History == During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} (313 – 399), according to temple legend, Ragyō Shōnin, a monk from India, came here following the river in search of a suitable place in which to practice his austerities and found [[Nachi Falls|Nachi waterfall]]. After practicing shugyō, Kannon appeared to him at the base of the waterfall and so he built a hermitage dedicated to Kannon in this place.
During the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592 – 628), Shōbutsu Shōnin came here from Yamato in order to undergo austerity practices. At that time he carved a 4 meter high image of Nyoirin Kannon from a single piece of camellia tree. The Hondō (main temple) was built to enshrine this image which became the focus of the Nachi Kannon cult and is the image that is enshrined in the present Nyorindō.
In 988 {{ill|Emperor Kazan|en|Emperor Kazan|WD=Q361000|lt=Emperor Kazan|1=Emperor Kazan}} (花山天皇) (968-1008) visited the Kumano area on his first pilgrimage and, being deeply moved by the image of Kannon, he declared this Temple One of the Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage. It is said that Emperor Kazan completed 1000 days of severe spiritual training under the waterfall, after which he had a vision of Kannon in the form of the kami Kumano Gongen. The kami instructed the emperor to find the priest Butsugan of Hasedera (Temple 8 of the pilgrimage), who helped the emperor to remap out the current pilgrimage route.
Emperor Kazan wrote all the goeika poems that are still used throughout the pilgrimage as sacred hymns. It became a custom in later years for other emperors who went on this pilgrimage to also compose poems of their own for each of the sacred sites.
Because [[:en:Emperor Gotoba of Japan|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239; r. 1183–1198) made the pilgrimage to Kumano 31 times and his successor {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} (1127 – 1192; reigned 1155 – 1158) made it 34 times, the pilgrimage became popular during this era. However, members of court had been coming here for about 400 years prior to this, believing it was near to Kannon's paradise island located to the south of Japan called Fudaraku (Potala in Sanskrit).
The temple buildings, like many of the temples on the pilgrimage route, were burned to the ground by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} during the civil wars of the 16th century. The Nyorindō (Main Temple) was rebuilt in 1587 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the Imperial Regent who unified Japan after the wars. It is typical of [[:en:Momoyama era|Momoyama Era]] style of architecture with a shingled distinctive roof called irimoya. The Nyorindō is heritage listed as a nationally Important Cultural Property.
When the government reinstated the power of the Emperor during the Meiji era (1868 - 1912), an attempt was made to separate Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines that had for over a thousand years shared the same grounds. Because of this, Seigantoji was formally abolished, the priests stripped of their authority and no funding was given to the temple. At that time Seigantoji had three main temples as well as 37 residential and training building. But all that remained after the Meiji Restoration was the Nyorindō and the abbot's quarters. However, gradually over the following century it was slowly rebuilt due to its position as an important part of the Kumano-Nachi syncretic mountain veneration religion of Shugendō.
In 1918, a {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} mound was excavated at the base of the waterfall and found to contain many important archaeological artifacts, including statues, mirrors, altar fittings and Sutra cylinders. These are now displayed in the Ryuhoden (“Treasure Hall”), located next to the Pagoda. These Sutra mounds were created by priests in times of war to hide their treasures but also many items were buried in this way as a result of the belief that the {{ill|Eschatology|en|Eschatology|WD=Q170337|lt=end of the world|1=Eschatology}} was coming at the start of the 10th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/History.htm|title = History of Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
== Buildings and other places of interest within the temple grounds == * Daimonsaka Forest Path * Sanmon (Mountain Gate) * [[Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha shrine]] (heiden) * Homotsuden Treasure Hall * Nyoirindō (The Temple of the Wish-fulfilling Kannon), the main temple (Hondō) * Sanjūdō pagoda (3-storey pagoda) * Nachi-no-Taki (Nachi Waterfall) * Nyohōdō (Hall of Lantern) * Sonsho-in Abbot's Quarters * Waniguchi "Crocodile Mouth" Gong * [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Places.htm|title = Places of Interest at Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>

[https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142bis 1142bis] }} {{nihongo|'''Seiganto-ji'''|青岸渡寺|1=Seiganto-ji|qq=draft}}, Temple of Crossing the Blue Shore, is a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was designated a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} in 2004 as part of {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}. According to a legend, it was founded by [[Ragyō Shōnin]], a monk from {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. The temple was purposely built near [[Nachi Falls]], where it may have previously been a site of nature worship. Seiganto-ji, part of the ''[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]]'' shrine complex, is one of the few remaining ''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}'' or [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|shrine temples]] following the forcible separation of Shinto and Buddhism during the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Jungūji|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1126|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=29 September 2010}}</ref>
It is the first stop on the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}} and is also classified as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the Japanese government.{{Citation needed|date=March 2024}}
== History == During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} (313 – 399), according to temple legend, Ragyō Shōnin, a monk from India, came here following the river in search of a suitable place in which to practice his austerities and found [[Nachi Falls|Nachi waterfall]]. After practicing shugyō, Kannon appeared to him at the base of the waterfall and so he built a hermitage dedicated to Kannon in this place.
During the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592 – 628), Shōbutsu Shōnin came here from Yamato in order to undergo austerity practices. At that time he carved a 4 meter high image of Nyoirin Kannon from a single piece of camellia tree. The Hondō (main temple) was built to enshrine this image which became the focus of the Nachi Kannon cult and is the image that is enshrined in the present Nyorindō.
In 988 {{ill|Emperor Kazan|en|Emperor Kazan|WD=Q361000|lt=Emperor Kazan|1=Emperor Kazan}} (花山天皇) (968-1008) visited the Kumano area on his first pilgrimage and, being deeply moved by the image of Kannon, he declared this Temple One of the Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage. It is said that Emperor Kazan completed 1000 days of severe spiritual training under the waterfall, after which he had a vision of Kannon in the form of the kami Kumano Gongen. The kami instructed the emperor to find the priest Butsugan of Hasedera (Temple 8 of the pilgrimage), who helped the emperor to remap out the current pilgrimage route.
Emperor Kazan wrote all the goeika poems that are still used throughout the pilgrimage as sacred hymns. It became a custom in later years for other emperors who went on this pilgrimage to also compose poems of their own for each of the sacred sites.
Because [[:en:Emperor Gotoba of Japan|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239; r. 1183–1198) made the pilgrimage to Kumano 31 times and his successor {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} (1127 – 1192; reigned 1155 – 1158) made it 34 times, the pilgrimage became popular during this era. However, members of court had been coming here for about 400 years prior to this, believing it was near to Kannon's paradise island located to the south of Japan called Fudaraku (Potala in Sanskrit).
The temple buildings, like many of the temples on the pilgrimage route, were burned to the ground by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} during the civil wars of the 16th century. The Nyorindō (Main Temple) was rebuilt in 1587 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the Imperial Regent who unified Japan after the wars. It is typical of [[:en:Momoyama era|Momoyama Era]] style of architecture with a shingled distinctive roof called irimoya. The Nyorindō is heritage listed as a nationally Important Cultural Property.
When the government reinstated the power of the Emperor during the Meiji era (1868 - 1912), an attempt was made to separate Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines that had for over a thousand years shared the same grounds. Because of this, Seigantoji was formally abolished, the priests stripped of their authority and no funding was given to the temple. At that time Seigantoji had three main temples as well as 37 residential and training building. But all that remained after the Meiji Restoration was the Nyorindō and the abbot's quarters. However, gradually over the following century it was slowly rebuilt due to its position as an important part of the Kumano-Nachi syncretic mountain veneration religion of Shugendō.
In 1918, a {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} mound was excavated at the base of the waterfall and found to contain many important archaeological artifacts, including statues, mirrors, altar fittings and Sutra cylinders. These are now displayed in the Ryuhoden (“Treasure Hall”), located next to the Pagoda. These Sutra mounds were created by priests in times of war to hide their treasures but also many items were buried in this way as a result of the belief that the {{ill|Eschatology|en|Eschatology|WD=Q170337|lt=end of the world|1=Eschatology}} was coming at the start of the 10th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/History.htm|title = History of Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
== Buildings and other places of interest within the temple grounds == * Daimonsaka Forest Path * Sanmon (Mountain Gate) * [[Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha shrine]] (heiden) * Homotsuden Treasure Hall * Nyoirindō (The Temple of the Wish-fulfilling Kannon), the main temple (Hondō) * Sanjūdō pagoda (3-storey pagoda) * Nachi-no-Taki (Nachi Waterfall) * Nyohōdō (Hall of Lantern) * Sonsho-in Abbot's Quarters * Waniguchi "Crocodile Mouth" Gong * [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Places.htm|title = Places of Interest at Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>

Committee}})</small> | designation1_free1name = Part of | designation1_free1value = {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}} | designation1_number = [https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1142bis 1142bis] }} {{nihongo|'''Seiganto-ji'''|青岸渡寺|1=Seiganto-ji|qq=draft}}, Temple of Crossing the Blue Shore, is a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} temple in {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was designated a {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|WD=Q9259|lt=UNESCO World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}} in 2004 as part of {{ill|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|en|Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|WD=Q611345|lt=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range|1=Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes in the Kii Mountain Range}}. According to a legend, it was founded by [[Ragyō Shōnin]], a monk from {{ill|India|en|India|WD=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. The temple was purposely built near [[Nachi Falls]], where it may have previously been a site of nature worship. Seiganto-ji, part of the ''[[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]]'' shrine complex, is one of the few remaining ''{{ill|Jingū-ji|en|Jingū-ji|WD=Q1074971|lt=Jingū-ji|1=Jingū-ji}}'' or [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|shrine temples]] following the forcible separation of Shinto and Buddhism during the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Jungūji|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1126|publisher=Encyclopedia of Shinto|accessdate=29 September 2010}}</ref>
It is the first stop on the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}} and is also classified as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}} by the Japanese government.{{Citation needed|date=March 2024}}
== History == During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|WD=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} (313 – 399), according to temple legend, Ragyō Shōnin, a monk from India, came here following the river in search of a suitable place in which to practice his austerities and found [[Nachi Falls|Nachi waterfall]]. After practicing shugyō, Kannon appeared to him at the base of the waterfall and so he built a hermitage dedicated to Kannon in this place.
During the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}} (592 – 628), Shōbutsu Shōnin came here from Yamato in order to undergo austerity practices. At that time he carved a 4 meter high image of Nyoirin Kannon from a single piece of camellia tree. The Hondō (main temple) was built to enshrine this image which became the focus of the Nachi Kannon cult and is the image that is enshrined in the present Nyorindō.
In 988 {{ill|Emperor Kazan|en|Emperor Kazan|WD=Q361000|lt=Emperor Kazan|1=Emperor Kazan}} (花山天皇) (968-1008) visited the Kumano area on his first pilgrimage and, being deeply moved by the image of Kannon, he declared this Temple One of the Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage. It is said that Emperor Kazan completed 1000 days of severe spiritual training under the waterfall, after which he had a vision of Kannon in the form of the kami Kumano Gongen. The kami instructed the emperor to find the priest Butsugan of Hasedera (Temple 8 of the pilgrimage), who helped the emperor to remap out the current pilgrimage route.
Emperor Kazan wrote all the goeika poems that are still used throughout the pilgrimage as sacred hymns. It became a custom in later years for other emperors who went on this pilgrimage to also compose poems of their own for each of the sacred sites.
Because [[:en:Emperor Gotoba of Japan|Emperor Gotoba]] (1180-1239; r. 1183–1198) made the pilgrimage to Kumano 31 times and his successor {{ill|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|en|Emperor Go-Shirakawa|WD=Q317984|lt=Emperor Go-Shirakawa|1=Emperor Go-Shirakawa}} (1127 – 1192; reigned 1155 – 1158) made it 34 times, the pilgrimage became popular during this era. However, members of court had been coming here for about 400 years prior to this, believing it was near to Kannon's paradise island located to the south of Japan called Fudaraku (Potala in Sanskrit).
The temple buildings, like many of the temples on the pilgrimage route, were burned to the ground by {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} during the civil wars of the 16th century. The Nyorindō (Main Temple) was rebuilt in 1587 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the Imperial Regent who unified Japan after the wars. It is typical of [[:en:Momoyama era|Momoyama Era]] style of architecture with a shingled distinctive roof called irimoya. The Nyorindō is heritage listed as a nationally Important Cultural Property.
When the government reinstated the power of the Emperor during the Meiji era (1868 - 1912), an attempt was made to separate Buddhist temples and Shinto shrines that had for over a thousand years shared the same grounds. Because of this, Seigantoji was formally abolished, the priests stripped of their authority and no funding was given to the temple. At that time Seigantoji had three main temples as well as 37 residential and training building. But all that remained after the Meiji Restoration was the Nyorindō and the abbot's quarters. However, gradually over the following century it was slowly rebuilt due to its position as an important part of the Kumano-Nachi syncretic mountain veneration religion of Shugendō.
In 1918, a {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|WD=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} mound was excavated at the base of the waterfall and found to contain many important archaeological artifacts, including statues, mirrors, altar fittings and Sutra cylinders. These are now displayed in the Ryuhoden (“Treasure Hall”), located next to the Pagoda. These Sutra mounds were created by priests in times of war to hide their treasures but also many items were buried in this way as a result of the belief that the {{ill|Eschatology|en|Eschatology|WD=Q170337|lt=end of the world|1=Eschatology}} was coming at the start of the 10th century.<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/History.htm|title = History of Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
== Buildings and other places of interest within the temple grounds == * Daimonsaka Forest Path * Sanmon (Mountain Gate) * [[Kumano Nachi Taisha|Kumano Nachi Taisha shrine]] (heiden) * Homotsuden Treasure Hall * Nyoirindō (The Temple of the Wish-fulfilling Kannon), the main temple (Hondō) * Sanjūdō pagoda (3-storey pagoda) * Nachi-no-Taki (Nachi Waterfall) * Nyohōdō (Hall of Lantern) * Sonsho-in Abbot's Quarters * Waniguchi "Crocodile Mouth" Gong * [[Fudarakusan-ji]]<ref>{{Cite web|url = http://sacredjapan.com/Temple%2001/Places.htm|title = Places of Interest at Seigantoji|date = |accessdate = October 9, 2014|website = Sacred Japan|publisher = |last = Juno|first = Cate Kodo}}</ref>
